This applies\n1244:     recursively for nested structures. This is how structure assignment worked\n1245:     in numpy >= 1.6 to <= 1.13.\n1246: \n1247:     Parameters\n1248:     ----------\n1249:     dst : ndarray\n1250:     src : ndarray\n1251:         The source and destination arrays during assignment.\n1252:     zero_unassigned : bool, optional\n1253:         If True, fields in the dst for which there was no matching\n1254:         field in the src are filled with the value 0 (zero). This\n1255:         was the behavior of numpy <= 1.13. If False, those fields\n1256:         are not modified.\n1257:     \"\"\"\n1258: \n1259:     if dst.dtype.names is None:\n1260:         dst[...] = src\n1261:         return\n1262: \n1263:     for name in dst.dtype.names:\n1264:         if name not in src.dtype.names:\n1265:             if zero_unassigned:\n1266:                 dst[name] = 0\n1267:         else:\n1268:             assign_fields_by_name(dst[name], src[name],\n1269:                                   zero_unassigned)\n1270: \n1271: def _require_fields_dispatcher(array, required_dtype):\n1272:     return (array,)\n1273: \n1274: @array_function_dispatch(_require_fields_dispatcher)\n1275: def require_fields(array, required_dtype):\n1276:     \"\"\"\n1277:     Casts a structured array to a new dtype using assignment by field-name.\n1278: \n1279:     This function assigns from the old to the new array by name, so the\n1280:     value of a field in the output array is the value of the field with the\n1281:     same name in the source array. the\n1285:     input array, that field is created and set to 0 in the output array.\n1286: \n1287:     Parameters\n1288:     ----------\n1289:     a : ndarray\n1290:        array to cast\n1291: _find_duplicates_dispatcher(\n1414:         a, key=None, ignoremask=None, return_index=None):\n1415:     return (a,)\n1416: \n1417: \n1418: @array_function_dispatch(_find_duplicates_dispatcher)\n1419: def find_duplicates(a, key=None, ignoremask=True, return_index=False):\n1420:     \"\"\"\n1421:     Find the duplicates in a structured array along a given key\n1422: \n1423:     Parameters\n1424:     ----------\n1425:     a : array-like\n1426:         Input array\n1427:     key : {string, None}, optional\n1428:         Name of the fields along which to check the duplicates.\n1429:         If None, the search is performed by records\n1430:     ignoremask : {True, False}, optional\n1431:         Whether masked data should be discarded or considered as duplicates.\n1432:     return_index : {False, True}, optional\n1433:         Whether to return the indices of the duplicated values.\n1434: \n1435:     Examples\n1436:     --------\n1437:     >>> import numpy as np\n1438:     >>> from numpy.lib import recfunctions as rfn\n1439:     >>> ndtype = [('a', int)]\n1440:     >>> a = np.ma.array([1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3],\n1441:     ...         mask=[0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1]).view(ndtype)\n1442:     >>> rfn.find_duplicates(a, ignoremask=True, return_index=True)\n1443:     (masked_array(data=[(1,), (1,), (2,), (2,)],\n1444:                  mask=[(False,), (False,), (False,), (False,)],\n1445: fields = get_fieldstructure(a.dtype)\n1451:     # Get the sorting data (by selecting the corresponding field)\n1452:     base = a\n1453:     if key:\n1454:         for f in fields[key]:\n1455:             base = base[f]\n1456:         base = base[key]\n1457:     # Get the sorting indices and the sorted data\n1458:     sortidx = base.argsort()\n1459:     sortedbase = base[sortidx]\n1460:     sorteddata = sortedbase.filled()\n1461:     # Compare the sorting data\n1462:     flag = (sorteddata[:-1] == sorteddata[1:])\n1463:     # If masked data must be ignored, set the flag to false where needed\n1464:     if ignoremask:\n1465:         sortedmask = sortedbase.recordmask\n1466:         flag[sortedmask[1:]] = False\n1467:     flag = np.concatenate(([False], flag))\n1468:     # We need to take the point on the left as well (else we're missing it)\n1469:     flag[:-1] = flag[:-1] + flag[1:]\n1470:     duplicates = a[sortidx][flag]\n1471:     if return_index:\n1472:         return (duplicates, sortidx[flag])\n1473:     else:\n1474:         return duplicates\n1475: \n1476: \n1477: def _join_by_dispatcher(\n1478:         key, r1, r2, jointype=None, r1postfix=None, r2postfix=None,\n1479:         defaults=None, usemask=None, asrecarray=None):\n1480:     return (r1, r2)\n1481: \n1482: \n1483: @array_function_dispatch(_join_by_dispatcher)\n1484: def join_by(key, r1, r2, jointype='inner', r1postfix='1', r2postfix='2',\n1485:             defaults=None, usemask=True, asrecarray=False):\n1486:     \"\"\"\n1487:     Join arrays `r1` and `r2` on key `key`.\n1488: \n1489:     The key should be either a string or a sequence of string corresponding\n1490:     to the fields used to join the array.  An exception is raised if the\n1491:     `key` field cannot be found in the two input arrays.  Neither `r1` nor\n1492:     `r2` should have any duplicates along `key`: the presence of duplicates\n1493:     will make the output quite unreliable. Note that duplicates are not\n1494:     looked for by the algorithm.\n1495: \n1496:     Parameters\n1497:     ----------\n1498:     key : {string, sequence}\n1499:         A string or a sequence of strings corresponding to the fields used\n1500:         for comparison.\n1501:     r1, r2 : arrays\n1502:         Structured arrays.\n1503:     jointype : {'inner', 'outer', 'leftouter'}, optional\n1504:         If 'inner', returns the elements common to both r1 and r2.\n1505:         If 'outer', returns the common elements as well as the elements of\n1506:         r1 not in r2 and the elements of not in r2.\n1507:         If 'leftouter', returns the common elements and the elements of r1\n1508:         not in r2.\n1509:     r1postfix : string, optional\n1510:         String appended to the names of the fields of r1 that are present\n1511:         in r2 but absent of the key.\n1512:     r2postfix : string, optional\n1513:         String appended to the names of the fields of r2 that are present\n1514:         in r1 but absent of the key.\n1515:     defaults : {dictionary}, optional\n1516:         Dictionary mapping field names to the corresponding default values.\n1517:     usemask : {True, False}, optional\n1518:         Whether to return a MaskedArray (or MaskedRecords is\n1519:         `asrecarray==True`) or a ndarray.\n1520:     asrecarray : {False, True}, optional\n1521:         Whether to return a recarray (or MaskedRecords if `usemask==True`)\n1522:         or just a flexible-type ndarray.\n1523: \n1524:     Notes\n1525:     -----\n1526:     * The output is sorted along the key.\n1527:     * A temporary array is formed by dropping the fields not in the key for\n1528:       the two arrays and concatenating the result. This array is then\n1529:       sorted, and the common entries selected. The output is constructed by\n1530:       filling the fields with the selected entries. Matching is not\n1531:       preserved if there are some duplicates...\n1532: \n1533:     \"\"\"\n1534:     # Check jointype\n1535:     if jointype not in ('inner', 'outer', 'leftouter'):\n1536:         raise ValueError(\n1537:                 \"The 'jointype' argument should be in 'inner', \"\n1538:                 \"'outer' or 'leftouter' (got '%s' instead)\" % jointype\n1539:                 )\n1540:     # If we have a single key, put it in a tuple\n1541:     if isinstance(key, str):\n1542:         key = (key,)\n1543: \n1544:     # Check the keys\n1545:     if len(set(key)) != len(key):\n1546:         dup = next(x for n,x in enumerate(key) if x in key[n+1:])\n1547:         raise ValueError(\"duplicate join key %r\" % dup)\n1548:     for name in key:\n1549:         if name not in r1.dtype.names:\n1550:             raise ValueError('r1 does not have key field %r' % name)\n1551:         if name not in r2.dtype.names:\n1552:             raise ValueError('r2 does not have key field %r' % name)\n1553: \n1554:     # Make sure we work with ravelled arrays\n1555:     r1 = r1.ravel()\n1556:     r2 = r2.ravel()\n1557:     # Fixme: nb2 below is never used. Commenting out for pyflakes.\n1558:     # (nb1, nb2) = (len(r1), len(r2))\n1559:     nb1 = len(r1)\n1560:     (r1names, r2names) = (r1.dtype.names, r2.dtype.names)\n1561: \n1562:     # Check the names for collision\n1563:     collisions = (set(r1names) & set(r2names)) - set(key)\n1564:     if collisions and not (r1postfix or r2postfix):\n1565:         msg = \"r1 and r2 contain common names, r1postfix and r2postfix \"\n1566:         msg += \"can't both be empty\"\n1567:         raise ValueError(msg)\n1568: \n1569:     # Make temporary arrays of just the keys\n1570:     #  (use order of keys in `r1` for back-compatibility)\n1571:     key1 = [ n for n in r1names if n in key ]\n1572:     r1k = _keep_fields(r1, key1)\n1573:     r2k = _keep_fields(r2, key1)\n1574: \n1575:     # Concatenate the two arrays for comparison\n1576:     aux = ma.concatenate((r1k, r2k))\n1577:     idx_sort = aux.argsort(order=key)\n1578:     aux = aux[idx_sort]\n1579:     #\n1580:     # Get the common keys\n1581:     flag_in = ma.concatenate(([False], aux[1:] == aux[:-1]))\n1582:     flag_in[:-1] = flag_in[1:] + flag_in[:-1]\n1583:     idx_in = idx_sort[flag_in]\n1584:     idx_1 = idx_in[(idx_in < nb1)]\n1585:     idx_2 = idx_in[(idx_in >= nb1)] - nb1\n1586:     (r1cmn, r2cmn) = (len(idx_1), len(idx_2))\n1587:     if jointype == 'inner':\n1588:         (r1spc, r2spc) = (0, 0)\n1589:     elif jointype == 'outer':\n1590:         idx_out = idx_sort[~flag_in]\n1591:         idx_1 = np.concatenate((idx_1, idx_out[(idx_out < nb1)]))\n1592:         idx_2 = np.concatenate((idx_2, idx_out[(idx_out >= nb1)] - nb1))\n1593:         (r1spc, r2spc) = (len(idx_1) - r1cmn, len(idx_2) - r2cmn)\n1594:     elif jointype == 'leftouter':\n1595:         idx_out = idx_sort[~flag_in]\n1596:         idx_1 = np.concatenate((idx_1, idx_out[(idx_out < nb1)]))\n1597:         (r1spc, r2spc) = (len(idx_1) - r1cmn, 0)\n1598:     # Select the entries from each input\n1599:     (s1, s2) = (r1[idx_1], r2[idx_2])\n1600:     #\n1601:     # Build the new description of the output array .......\n1602:     # Start with the key fields\n1603:     ndtype = _get_fieldspec(r1k.dtype)\n1604: \n1605:     # Add the fields from r1\n1606:     for fname, fdtype in _get_fieldspec(r1.dtype):\n1607:         if fname not in key:\n1608:             ndtype.append((fname, fdtype))\n1609: \n1610:     # Add the fields from r2\n1611:     for fname, fdtype in _get_fieldspec(r2.dtype):\n1612:         # Have we seen the current name already ?\n1613:         # we need to rebuild this list every time\n1614:         names = list(name for name, dtype in ndtype)\n1615:         try:\n1616:             nameidx = names.index(fname)\n1617:         except ValueError:\n1618:             #... we haven't: just add the description to the current list\n1619:             ndtype.append((fname, fdtype))\n1620:         else:\n1621:             # collision\n1622:             _, cdtype = ndtype[nameidx]\n1623:             if fname in key:\n1624:                 # The current field is part of the key: take the largest dtype\n1625:                 ndtype[nameidx] = (fname, max(fdtype, cdtype))\n1626:             else:\n1627:                 # The current field is not part of the key: add the suffixes,\n1628:                 # and place the new field adjacent to the old one\n1629:                 ndtype[nameidx:nameidx + 1] = [\n1630:                     (fname + r1postfix, cdtype),\n1631:                     (fname + r2postfix, fdtype)\n1632:                 ]\n1633:     # Rebuild a dtype from the new fields\n1634:     ndtype = np.dtype(ndtype)\n1635:     # Find the largest nb of common fields :\n1636:     # r1cmn and r2cmn should be equal, but...\n1637:     cmn = max(r1cmn, r2cmn)\n1638:     # Construct an empty array\n1639:     output = ma.masked_all((cmn + r1spc + r2spc,), dtype=ndtype)\n1640:     names = output.dtype.names\n1641:     for f in r1names:\n1642:         selected = s1[f]\n1643:         if f not in names or (f in r2names and not r2postfix and f not in key):\n1644:             f += r1postfix\n1645:         current = output[f]\n1646:         current[:r1cmn] = selected[:r1cmn]\n1647:         if jointype in ('outer', 'leftouter'):\n1648:             current[cmn:cmn + r1spc] = selected[r1cmn:]\n1649:     for f in r2names:\n1650:         selected = s2[f]\n1651:         if f not in names or (f in r1names and not r1postfix and f not in key):\n1652:             f += r2postfix\n1653:         current = output[f]\n1654:         current[:r2cmn] = selected[:r2cmn]\n1655:         if (jointype == 'outer') and r2spc:\n1656:             current[-r2spc:] = selected[r2cmn:]\n1657:     # Sort and finalize the output\n1658:     output.sort(order=key)\n1659:     kwargs = dict(usemask=usemask, asrecarray=asrecarray)\n1660:     return _fix_output(_fix_defaults(output, defaults), **kwargs)\n1661: \n1662: \n1663: def _rec_join_dispatcher(\n1664:         key, r1, r2, jointype=None, r1postfix=None, r2postfix=None,\n1665:         defaults=None):\n1666:     return (r1, r2)\n1667: \n1668: \n1669: @array_function_dispatch(_rec_join_dispatcher)\n1670: def rec_join(key, r1, r2, jointype='inner', r1postfix='1', r2postfix='2',\n1671:              defaults=None):\n1672:     \"\"\"\n1673:     Join arrays `r1` and `r2` on keys.\n1674:     Alternative to join_by, that always returns a np.recarray.\n1675: \n1676:     See Also\n1677:     --------\n1678:     join_by : equivalent function\n1679:     \"\"\"\n1680:     kwargs = dict(jointype=jointype, r1postfix=r1postfix, r2postfix=r2postfix,\n1681:                   defaults=defaults, usemask=False, asrecarray=True)\n1682:     return join_by(key, r1, r2, **kwargs)\n1683: \n1684: \n1685: del array_function_dispatch\n1686: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/lib/recfunctions.py"}}}
